title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Calcitonin gene-related peptide receptors (CGRP) receptor antagonists and monoclonal antibodies against CGRP and its receptor are medications used in the management and treatment of migraine, which is the sixth-highest cause worldwide of years lost due to disability as per the Global Burden of Disease Study (2013). This activity describes the indications, actions, and contraindications for these drugs as valuable agents in the prophylaxis and acute management of migraine and cluster headaches. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, monitoring, adverse events, relevant interactions, and other key elements in the clinical setting as relates to the essential points needed by members of an interprofessional team managing the care of patients with migraines.

title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: Since the studies regarding CGRP and its receptor, physiology, and pharmacology were conducted relatively recently, there is much unknown about it. The new drugs acting on the CGRP receptor have greater efficacy and advantages over the drugs currently used for the treatment of migraines, which may lead to the widespread use of these new drugs. However, professionals within the field of internal medicine and neurology have to take care while prescribing these drugs and properly inform patients about the potential side effects of these medications. Watching for adverse effects reported in previous studies and preparing for those that have not yet been described is also vital.

title: Migraine Medications -- Function -- Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide (CGRP)
content: CGRP monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) are the only class of currently used preventives explicitly developed for the treatment of migraines. The current thinking is that CGRP mediates the vasodilatory component of neurogenic inflammation, as CGRP is a widely distributed vasodilator. The CGRP mAbs target either the CGRP molecule itself or the CGRP receptor. In network meta-analysis, the CGRP mAbs seemed to be as effective as other preventive treatments, but have fewer side effects. [14] Long-term data on safety, however, is limited.  These medications include erenumab, fremanezumab, and galcanezumab.

title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Mechanism of Action
content: CGRP receptor antagonists ("gepants") are the first class of migraine-specific medication that does not have vasoconstrictive action, shows similar efficacy, has fewer adverse effects, and has a possibly longer period of activity than triptans. [7] Gepants demonstrate an extremely high affinity for CGRP receptors of human and non-human primates, preventing in this way the interaction between CGRP and its receptor. [12] The structured N-terminal extracellular (ECD) domain of CLR serves as a binding site for gepants, thus providing its fast-acting antagonistic action. The currently FDA-approved gepants are rimegepant and ubrogepant.

title: Biochemistry, Peptide -- Molecular Level -- Peptide Hormones
content: Calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) is a 37-amino acid neuropeptide, which is most commonly localized to C and Aδ sensory fibers but affects both the central and peripheral nervous systems and metabolism through a multitude of receptor types. [16] New research has demonstrated that intraperitoneal treatment with CGRP can have an energy stimulating effect and even promote an increased appetite. [17] Current research has also linked the activity CGRP in the cerebrovascular system to a possible etiology of migraine attacks. [18] CGRP is mainly found in the enteric nervous system but has been postulated to play a potential role in cranial nociception and cerebral vasodilation, leading to severe migraine headaches. [19]

title: Migraine With Aura -- Treatment / Management -- Ergots
content: Ergots bind to 5-HT 1B/1D receptors and act as vasoconstrictors by inhibiting dural plasma extravasation following trigeminal ganglion stimulation in rats and may centrally block the trigeminovascular pathway. The oral bioavailability of ergotamine is very poor. Ergots are effective via intravenous (IV), intramuscular (IM), subcutaneous, and intranasal routes. Dihydroergotamine has demonstrated efficacy in treating acute migraine attacks and serving as a bridge therapy for medication overuse headaches and status migrainosus. Nevertheless, further research is required to establish the full effectiveness of ergotamine.

title: Migraine Headache in Childhood -- Treatment / Management -- Recommended Preventive Treatment
content: Another study found that serum magnesium levels were significantly lower among children with migraine compared to those without migraine. Therefore, natural remedies such as magnesium, CoQ-10, ginkgolide B butterbur, riboflavin, polyunsaturated fatty acids, and oral melatonin have proven efficient and have a lower side effect profile. [58] Since most preventive migraine treatments modify the brain's excitation/inhibition balance or serotonin metabolism, they most likely produce unfavorable side effects. Biological therapies blocking CGRP transmission are effective and better tolerated; however, they are expensive and may not influence brain dysfunctions upstream in the pathophysiological cascade of migraine, including premonitory and aura symptoms. On the other hand, biochemical and clinical studies mentioned a complementary treatment strategy focusing on migraine pathophysiology's metabolic facet. Findings revealed that riboflavin and coenzyme Q10 are effective for migraine prevention and quasi-devoid of adverse effects. Their response rates are similar to those of topiramate, propranolol, and CGRP/CGRPrec mAbs but are weaker for thioctic acid. Therefore, metabolic treatments of migraine demonstrated an effective, well-tolerated, inexpensive, and evidence-supported therapeutic option for migraine prophylaxis and may be used as the first treatment line among children. [59]

title: Posttraumatic Headache -- Treatment / Management -- Migraine-type
content: Herbal and vitamin supplements have shown some benefits in managing migraine headaches. There is evidence that magnesium, feverfew, vitamin B2, CoQ10, and Petasites can help prevent migraine headaches. [28] Triptans are the mainstay treatment of acute migraines. [29] Other abortive treatments for migraine include acetaminophen, NSAIDs, butalbital-containing medications, ergot derivatives, and antiemetics. [30] Opioids are not recommended for acute migraine therapy. [31] Monoclonal antibodies to calcitonin gene-related peptides (CGRP-mAbs) were developed to prevent migraine headaches. Other prophylactic therapies include TCAs, the anticonvulsants topiramate and valproate, and beta-blockers. [32] Botulinum toxin injection is another treatment modality for the management of chronic migraines. [33]

Levels are elevated during a migraine attack and between migraine attacks in patients with chronic migraines. Also, CGRP from exogenous infusions was shown to trigger migraine attacks. title: Migraine Prophylaxis -- Function -- 1. Pharmacological Therapies for Migraine Prevention
content: Various medications are available for migraine prophylaxis and prevention. Choosing the right agent is essential. The efficacy, side effects, contraindications, cost, and compliance should be considered when deciding on the right agent. It is also crucial to consider comorbid medical conditions and drug interactions. Efficacy of treatment can be assessed only after a 2 to 3-month trial; a full trial may take up to 6 months. Always start at a low dose and titrate up slowly. Monotherapy is preferred as there are no significant benefits of using more than one drug unless indicated for other comorbid conditions. Patients should be re-evaluated, and medications preferably discontinued after one year, even if they show improvement in symptoms. Successful therapy is defined as a reduction of migraine attacks by at least 50%. [3] [4] [8]

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 335
content: Substance with peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action represents a broad class of pharmacologic agents that block the activity of peptide hormone receptors. Within this overarching category, Substance with receptor antagonist mechanism of action (substance) is identified as a more specific subclass of agents that exert their therapeutic effect by antagonizing receptors in general. A particular example of this subclass is antagonisté CGRP receptorů, which comprises compounds that specifically inhibit the calcitonin gene‑related peptide (CGRP) receptor. Thus, CGRP receptor antagonists are situated under the umbrella of receptor‑antagonist substances, which in turn are encompassed by the larger group of peptide‑hormone receptor antagonists. This hierarchical organization clarifies how these drug classes relate to one another and aids clinicians in understanding the scope of receptor‑targeted therapies.

title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
content: Antimigraine preparations comprise a diverse group of medications used to prevent or treat migraine attacks. This category includes the oral‑dose product of clonidine, other antimigraine agents classified in the ATC system, and specific drugs such as almotriptan, naratriptan, methysergid, atogepant, dichloralphenazone, lisurid, and pizotylin. These agents vary in their mechanisms of action—triptans like almotriptan, naratriptan, and methysergid act as serotonin receptor agonists, atogepant is a CGRP receptor antagonist, dichloralphenazone provides analgesic and sedative effects, and clonidine, lisurid, and pizotylin have additional vasoconstrictive or neuromodulatory properties. Together, they offer clinicians multiple therapeutic options for individualized migraine management and improve patient care by targeting different pathophysiological pathways involved in migraine.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
content: Icatibant is classified among the drugs used to treat hereditary angioedema, a rare genetic disorder characterized by episodic swelling of the skin and mucosal tissues. As a bradykinin B2‑receptor antagonist, icatibant specifically blocks the action of bradykinin, a key mediator of the swelling attacks in hereditary angioedema, thereby providing rapid relief of symptoms during acute episodes. Inclusion of icatibant in the therapeutic armamentarium underscores its role as a targeted pharmacologic option for managing this condition and improving patient outcomes.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2302
content: Migraine with ischemic complication is a broader clinical category that encompasses forms of migraine in which vascular ischemia occurs, and persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction is a specific manifestation within this category. Persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction is also classified as an acute cerebrovascular disease, reflecting its nature as a sudden, stroke‑like event caused by interruption of cerebral blood flow during a prolonged migraine aura. By linking migraine with ischemic complication to acute cerebrovascular disease through the specific entity of persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction, the knowledge underscores that certain prolonged migraine auras can evolve into acute cerebral infarctions, highlighting the importance of recognizing these migraine‑related ischemic complications for timely diagnosis and appropriate stroke‑focused management.

title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 68
content: Clonidine only product in oral dose form, other antimigraine preparations in ATC, almotriptan, atogepant, dichloralphenazone, flunarizin‑dihydrochlorid, lisurid, methysergid, and naratriptan are all classified as antimigraine preparations, representing a diverse group of pharmacologic agents used to prevent or treat migraine attacks. Within this therapeutic class, the subgroup known as “námelové alkaloidy” is identified as a child category, indicating that certain alkaloid‑derived compounds also belong to the broader antimigraine preparations family. By grouping these varied medications under the single umbrella of antimigraine preparations, clinicians can recognize their shared therapeutic purpose while also appreciating the distinct mechanisms and chemical structures—ranging from triptans (almotriptan, naratriptan) to gepants (atogepant) and other agents such as dichloralphenazone and flunarizin‑dihydrochlorid—that contribute to migraine management. title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 68
content: Antimigraine preparations comprise a diverse group of pharmacologic agents used to prevent or treat migraine attacks. Among these agents are oral clonidine products, which are classified as antimigraine preparations, as well as other antimigraine preparations listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system. Specific triptans such as almotriptan, naratriptan, and the zolmitriptan 2.5 mg oral tablet are also members of this class, and the zolmitriptan tablet contains the inactive ingredient hypromelóza. Hypromelóza, besides serving as an excipient in zolmitriptan, is linked to nasal vasoconstrictor formulations, reflecting its broader relationship to vasoconstrictive agents used in the nasal cavity. Additional antimigraine drugs include atogepant, dichloralphenazone, flunarizin‑dihydrochloride, lisurid, methysergid, and pizotylin, all of which are recognized as types of antimigraine preparations. The category further encompasses a sub‑class of alkaloid compounds referred to as “námelové alkaloidy,” highlighting the chemical diversity within migraine therapy. Together, these agents illustrate the wide range of mechanisms—ranging from serotonin receptor agonism to CGRP antagonism and vasoconstriction—employed to manage migraine, underscoring the importance of selecting appropriate formulations and excipients for optimal patient care.
